w:document.mc:Ignorable: w14 w15 w16se w16cid w16 w16cex w16sdtdh w16sdtfl w16du wp14

w:r.w:t: Authoritative Vocabulary Lists, State Transitions & Valid

This document is the authoritative reference for the six CBV 2.0 vocabulary lists that the track-and-trace platform uses throughout its EPCIS events: business steps, dispositions (transient and persistent), business transaction types, source/destination types, error reason codes, and sensor measurement types.

Where the four identifier specifications (GTIN, GLN, SGTIN, SSCC) and the EPCIS Events specification REFERENCE these values inline, this document DEFINES them — every value, its meaning, its typical pharma use, and its valid pairings with other values.

Why does this matter? CBV compliance is a formal GS1 conformance level: a CBV-Compliant EPCIS document uses ONLY CBV vocabulary values in standard fields. A CBV-Compatible document may mix in custom URIs but remains interoperable. Regulators (Tatmeen, EMVO, DSCSA) increasingly expect CBV-Compliant submissions. Without a frozen vocabulary reference, developers make ad-hoc choices that erode compliance.
All definitions are grounded in the GS1 CBV 2.0 Standard (Ratified June 2022, ISO/IEC 19988:2024), the EPCIS JSON-LD context (github.com/gs1/EPCIS/
), and the EPCIS + CBV Implementation Guideline 2.0 (March 2023). Where CBV 2.0 added new values over CBV 1.2, we note it.

The Core Business Vocabulary (CBV) is GS1's companion standard to EPCIS. EPCIS defines the data STRUCTURE of events (the envelope fields, the five event types, how they are serialised as JSON-LD or XML). The CBV defines the VALUES that populate those structures — the specific strings that go into the

Without CBV, every trading partner could invent their own strings for the same concept. Partner A says 'shipping', partner B says 'SHIPPED', partner C says 'depart'. None of these are interoperable. CBV is the agreement that everyone uses the same controlled vocabulary — so 'shipping' means the same thing across every pharma company, every wholesaler, every regulator, everywhere in the world.
: The EPCIS document uses ONLY CBV-defined vocabulary values in the standard fields. No custom URIs. The tightest conformance level. Required for

: The document uses CBV values where they exist but permits custom URIs (from a user-defined vocabulary) for concepts CBV doesn't cover. Still interoperable with CBV-aware consumers; they'll process the standard values and ignore (or log) the custom ones.

Our platform's default policy: EMIT CBV-Compliant output; INGEST CBV-Compatible input. That is, we ourselves only use CBV values, but we accept custom URIs from trading partners (with audit flagging) rather than reject their events. This maximises interoperability while preserving our own cleanliness.
Every CBV value has two equivalent URI forms. CBV 2.0 accepts both; internally we normalise to the Web URI form (EPCIS 2.0's preferred syntax).
Section 3: Dispositions — the ~32 standard disposition values with pharma usage.
Section 4: Business Transaction Types — the 13 standard
Section 6: Error Reason Codes — CBV 2.0's new
× disposition pairings — the canonical matrix for what disposition can follow what
. Single most useful reference for UI design and validation.
Section 9: Pharma pack lifecycle state machine — from commissioning through dispensing with all valid transitions.
Section 10: Vocabulary Management — how the platform handles user-vocabulary extensions, version transitions, and validation.
field answers the WHY dimension of an EPCIS event — specifically, what step of the business process was occurring. Practically every EPCIS event should have a
value. CBV 2.0 defines 41 standard values. Each event carries EXACTLY ONE
A specific activity within a business process where an object changes possession or ownership or both.
Wholesaler accepts delivery from manufacturer; custody passes from shipper to receiver.
A specific activity within a business process where an object arrives at a location.
Pallet arrives at the wholesaler's receiving dock before being formally received/accepted.
An activity within a business process whereby one or more objects are combined to create a new finished product.
Combining components into a diagnostic test kit.
A specific activity within a business process where an object is picked up and collected for future disposal, recycling, or re-use.
Reverse-logistics collector picks up expired medications from a pharmacy for destruction.

An activity that assigns a unique identifier to an object — either an instance-level identifier encoded on a tag/label for the first time, or a class-level identifier associated with instance-level identifiers for the first time.
Serialised pack leaves the packaging line with a
Data Matrix; the SGTIN instance comes into existence.
, loading, departing, and accepting (the composite outbound shipping flow).
Seller-side umbrella process for preparing and despatching goods; often emitted as a summary event.
An activity that creates an instance of a specific class of items.
A new batch/lot of a product is declared and associated with serial-level objects that will be commissioned.
A specific activity within a business process where an inventory check is performed on a cyclical basis.
Pharmacy performs weekly physical count of controlled substances.
Retiring or removing items from active use. The inverse of commissioning.
Sample packs decommissioned at end of clinical trial.
Goods leaving a location (a truck leaves the
Truck with cold-chain shipment departs the manufacturer's dispatch dock.
Expired or recalled product is incinerated under regulator witness.
Separating a composite object into its constituent components.
Breaking down a diagnostic kit into its sub-components.
A specific activity in the healthcare context where an object is provided to the consumer of the product. (Added in CBV 1.2 for pharma.)
Pharmacy hands pack to patient; the SGTIN's life ends at this event.
Process of writing an instance-level identifier (typically an EPC) to a barcode or RFID tag, where the identifier isn't yet associated with an object at this step. Must only be used in a
Serial-number pool loaded into the packaging line's encoder prior to commissioning.
Denotes a specific activity at the Entrance/Exit door of a facility where customers are leaving with purchased product or entering with product to be returned.
Pharmacy door RFID scan when a pack physically crosses the threshold (used in some retail-pharmacy models).
Denotes a specific activity within a business process where an object is separated for further review.
Quality Assurance isolates a batch for investigation before release.
Process of reviewing objects to address potential physical or documentation defects.
Customs inspection of imported pharmaceutical shipment.
Denotes a specific activity within a business process where an object is put into a composite object (not merely a container). Often used with
Datalogger glued onto cold-chain pallet; sensor mounted in cold storage room.
Process of terminating an RFID tag previously associated with an object. The object itself may continue to exist under a different identifier (e.g., barcode, replacement tag).
Rare in pharma (pharma uses 2D Data Matrix, not RFID, at pack level).
Forklift loads cold-chain pallets into a reefer truck.
An undefined or custom business step. Use sparingly — prefer extending via user vocabulary URIs.
Fallback; avoid when a specific CBV value fits.
Preparing goods for shipment; physically aggregating units into a case or cases into a pallet.
Saleable packs aggregated into a case (
Selecting goods for orders from inventory.
Warehouse picker selects packs against a wholesaler order.
Taking delivery of goods and recognising them in inventory.
Wholesaler receives pallet; custody passes to the receiver.
Stock pull from shelf prior to dispensing or disposal.
Altering the packaging of goods. Often irreversible — inputs destroyed, outputs emerge. Critical for pharma parallel-trade and kitting.
Licensed Agent in UAE opens imported packs, relabels in Arabic, issues new SGTINs under own GCP.
Restoring an object to operational or acceptable condition.
Limited pharma use; more common with medical devices.
Swap-out of damaged pack within a shipment.
Setting an object aside for a specific future purpose, making it unavailable for general use.
Stock reserved against a pending government tender.
Consumer purchases the item; the final point-of-sale in B2C retail.
Object sent back for various reasons — expired, damaged, excess, recalled.
Pharmacy returns expired pack to distributor; distributor may then destroy.
Taking a sample for testing, quality control, or analysis.
QA pulls samples from a batch for release testing.
Reporting of sensor measurements. Primary vehicle for cold-chain telemetry in EPCIS 2.0.
Datalogger posts temperature readings every 15 minutes during transport.
Goods physically leave the shipper on their way to the consignee.
Pallet leaves manufacturer's dispatch dock; custody in transit.
Preparing goods in a staging area pending actual shipment.
Outbound packs staged at dock pending truck arrival.
Counting inventory in storage (periodic full inventory, distinct from
Annual full inventory across pharmacy chain.
Pack placed on pharmacy shelf after receiving and unpacking.
Keeping goods in a warehouse or facility over time.
Ongoing warehouse hold; often emits periodic
Moving goods between locations — the movement itself, as opposed to discrete shipping/receiving events.
Continuous-observation event during long-haul cold-chain transit.
Forklift takes pallets off the arriving reefer truck.
Opening packages and removing contents; disaggregating a logistic unit or case.
Pallet broken down at pharmacy-distribution centre; contained cases emerge.
Cancelling a shipment that had been declared shipped. (Added in CBV 1.2 for pharma DSCSA contexts.)
Shipment was voided before arriving at destination — regulatory correction.
The disposition field answers: what is the business condition of the objects AFTER this event? Usually an adjective — 'active', '
is the verb (what was DONE), disposition is the adjective (what STATE did the object end up in).
CBV 2.0 distinguishes two flavours of disposition:
Transient (the disposition field): reflects the state
of the event. Overridden by the next event's disposition on the same object. Example: '

field, new in EPCIS 2.0): sticks to the object until EXPLICITLY unset. Example: once 'damaged' is set persistently, the object remains damaged through subsequent events — even if the transient disposition moves to '
', the persistent 'damaged' is still true. An unset event explicitly removes it.
Both fields draw from the SAME vocabulary — the list below. In practice most dispositions are used transiently; a handful (damaged, recalled, stolen) are almost always used persistently.
A new commissioned object has been introduced to the supply chain.
Set on commissioning events. The default live state for a serialised pack.
Object has been returned to service or to the supply chain.
Used after an issue (e.g., quarantine) is resolved and the object returns to normal flow.

A downstream recipient infers that the contents of a shipment below the outer packing layer are complete, based on upstream EPCIS packing/shipping events — without physically verifying each contained object.
Used when a trusted upstream supplier has provided full aggregation events; the receiver accepts without scanning every child SGTIN.
A downstream recipient verifies that the contents of a shipment below the outer packing layer are complete. Verification requires physical unpacking and scanning each contained object.
Used when the receiver physically scans each pack in the case/pallet at receipt.
Outcome of a successful, passed inspection in the inspecting or repairing step.
Set after a QA inspection passes (e.g., successful release testing).
Object has been loaded onto a container, the doors have been closed, and the shipment has been sealed.
Shipping-container-level event — shipment is sealed and ready to depart.
or a seal of a shipment has been broken.
Important for controlled substances and anti-tamper verification on receipt.
An object has been damaged or reduced in value.
Persistent disposition — once damaged, remains damaged until explicitly unset.
The object has been destroyed, typically as the outcome of a destroying event.
The object has been dispensed to a patient or end consumer. (Added in CBV 1.2 for pharma.)
= dispensing and action = DELETE. The SGTIN's life ends.
Object has been disposed of — e.g., sent for recycling or waste disposal, as distinct from active destruction.
End-of-life but distinct from 'destroyed' — destroyed is more final.
Encoding of an identifier onto a tag has been completed, but the object has not yet been commissioned.
Object has passed its expiry date.
Critical for pharma. Platform must auto-flag on expiry date + 1 day. Non-sellable, non-dispensable.
Objects are moving normally through the supply chain. The workhorse disposition — default for most events that don't require a more specific state.
Note: CBV 2.0 recommends OMITTING this disposition when the state is the normal one — its meaning is a default, not an explicit signal.
Object is moving between locations as part of an active shipment.
Set on shipping events; cleared on receiving events.
Object is not currently in active use in the supply chain.
Used after decommissioning without destruction (e.g., retired product withdrawn from market).
The received object does not match the class of item expected (e.g., wrong GTIN delivered).
Receiving discrepancy — flags a shipment-vs-expectation mismatch.
The received instance does not match the expected instance (e.g., wrong serial number delivered).
Receiving discrepancy at the serialised-pack level.
The received quantity does not match the expected quantity.
Component(s) or other asset(s) must be replaced to ensure fulfilment of functional requirements.
Rare in pharma pack flows; more relevant for returnable-asset tracking.
Set after a QA inspection fails; downstream flow is blocked pending resolution.
Object cannot be sold for an unspecified reason. Used as a general 'not for sale' state when a more specific disposition doesn't apply.
Catch-all for non-sellable state. Prefer a more specific disposition (expired, damaged, recalled) when applicable.
A portion of a product has been dispensed to a consumer; additional product is retained for later dispensing. (Added in CBV 1.2 for pharma.)
Used for pack split-dispense scenarios (e.g., patient-specific unit-dose dispensing from a larger pack).
Object is subject to a regulatory or manufacturer-initiated recall.
Persistent disposition. Critical for reverse-logistics workflows. Paired with
Object has been set aside for a specific future use or customer, making it unavailable for other purposes.
Used in tender-allocation workflows and hospital-reserved stock.
Object has been sold at retail to the end consumer.
Terminal for OTC pharma. Equivalent in meaning to 'dispensed' for consumer sale channels.
Object has been sent or brought back — reason varies (excess stock, damage, expiry, recall).
= returning. May be followed by further events (destroyed, re-dispensed).
Object is sellable AND accessible by the customer (e.g., on shelf in a retail store).
OTC pharma on the pharmacy shelf.
Object is sellable as-is, but not accessible by the customer for self-service purchase (e.g., behind-the-counter, pharmacist-dispensed).
The canonical state for Rx pharma —
Object has been taken without permission or right.
Persistent disposition. Mandatory report to DEA for US controlled substances; to MoHAP for UAE narcotics.
Object has been removed from service or from the supply chain (e.g., pending repair, under inspection).
Temporary state during QA inspection or quarantine.
The disposition is not known at the time of the event.
Use sparingly. Indicates a data-quality issue worth flagging for review.
While any disposition CAN be used persistently, pharma practice limits persistent use to states that should remain true until explicitly negated:
damaged — remains true until a repair or a retirement event explicitly
recalled — remains true for the lifetime of the object (never unset without regulator sign-off).
stolen — remains true until the object is recovered and formally restored.
expired — remains true once the expiry date has passed (never unset).
— remain true to convey the verified state of an aggregated shipment
Setting these transiently is a common data-quality issue; the platform's validation recommends 'persistent' for these values and flags transient usage for review.

field on an EPCIS event holds a list of references to business documents relevant to the event. Each entry is a pair: a TYPE (from the vocabulary below) plus an IDENTIFIER (a URI naming the specific document — typically a customer-assigned reference).
— linking the shipment to both the purchase order and the dispatch advice.
Bill of Lading. A document issued by a carrier to a shipper, listing and acknowledging receipt of goods for transport and specifying terms of delivery.
Ocean/air carrier BOL for international pharma shipment.
Certificate. A document confirming certain characteristics of an object, party, or location (typically issued by a third party).
GMP certificate linked to a manufacturing facility; Certificate of Analysis attached to a batch.
Dispatch Advice / Advance Ship Notice (ASN). A document by which the seller informs the consignee about the dispatch of goods. The value '
' is used regardless of local nomenclature.
ASN sent from manufacturer to wholesaler ahead of pallet arrival. Universal in pharma flows.
Invoice. A document claiming payment for goods or services supplied under agreed conditions.
Commercial invoice attached to the pharma sales transaction.
A record that traces the ownership and transactions of a product as it moves among various trading partners.
Legacy DSCSA-era pedigree document. Still relevant for US state-level requirements and some emerging-market regulations.
Purchase Order. A buyer's document requesting the sale of goods/services.
Purchase Order Confirmation. Seller's confirmation of a buyer's PO. Also used as 'Sales Order' (CBV 2.0 clarification).
Production Order. An internal document ordering a production run.
Batch production order linking manufacturing events to the underlying order.
Receiving Advice. A document from the receiver to the shipper reporting actual goods received vs what was advised.
Wholesaler's RECADV back to manufacturer confirming the ASN was fulfilled correctly (or noting discrepancies).
Return Merchandise Authorisation. Authorisation for a return of goods.
Distributor's authorisation to a pharmacy for return of over-stocked product.
Test Procedure. A document describing a testing procedure.
Release test procedure linked to a batch's release event.
Test Results. A document reporting the results of a testing procedure.
Certificate of Analysis (CoA) results for a batch.
Upstream EPCIS Event. Event ID URI(s) of event(s) provided by an upstream supplier — used to link to another event (e.g., as the basis for a
When a receiver trusts an upstream packing event, that event's ID is linked via
so downstream events can reference it.
fields model custody transfer. Each entry is a pair: a TYPE (from this short list) plus an IDENTIFIER (typically a PGLN or SGLN URI).
These three types together distinguish three distinct kinds of transfer: WHO LEGALLY OWNS (
), and WHERE IT IS (location). In pharma, all three often differ — a Manufacturer owns goods that a 3PL physically holds at a distribution centre's location.
The party that owns the object at the source or destination — legal ownership.
Identifies the legal owner. Uses PGLN URI form (https://id.gs1.org/417/<GLN>). Preferred form per CBV 2.0.
The party with physical possession of the object at the source or destination, which may differ from the owner (e.g., a 3PL physically holds but doesn't own).
Identifies the physical custodian. Uses PGLN URI form. Critical for 3PL / bonded-warehouse / licensed-agent scenarios.
The physical location at the source or destination.
Uses SGLN URI form (https://id.gs1.org/414/<GLN>). Must be consistent with
Example: manufacturer ships via 3PL to wholesaler
At a shipping event from manufacturer to a 3PL-run cross-dock, destined for the wholesaler:
did NOT change — the 3PL only took physical possession. A separate event at wholesaler-receipt will then transfer
event turns out to have been wrong, EPCIS specifies the error-declaration mechanism: a new event is issued containing the same content as the erroneous one PLUS an
carries a reason code (optional but strongly recommended) from the vocabulary below.
This vocabulary was NEW in CBV 2.0. Before CBV 2.0,
was a free-text field. Standardising it makes programmatic handling of error declarations possible.
The event declared in error did not actually occur. The prior event was submitted by mistake or based on faulty information.
Destruction event was reported, but later it was discovered the physical destruction never took place — the product is still in the warehouse.
The event occurred, but one or more data fields on the prior event were recorded incorrectly. Corrective events should be issued with the correct data.
Shipping event captured with the wrong destination GLN; the shipment did
but the event data misrepresents where it went.
The reason for the error does not fit the other standard categories. A textual explanation SHOULD accompany the error declaration.
Step 1: The ORIGINAL event remains in the log, unchanged. EPCIS events are immutable — even erroneous ones.
Step 2: A NEW event is issued containing all the same fields as the original PLUS an

list points to separate NEW events that set the record straight with correct data. The platform validates these forward links but does not auto-apply the corrections to projections — that requires explicit corrective events.
Step 4: All events (original, declaration, corrective) remain in the log forever. Queries against 'the current truth' must consider
field for the HOW dimension — carrying sensor measurements alongside events. Each
entry has a `type` naming what is being measured.
CBV 2.0 introduced a standard vocabulary of sensor types. The most important for pharma
are Temperature and Humidity. Units of measure come from UN/CEFACT Recommendation 20 — CEL for Celsius, P1 for percentage, etc.
Cold-chain compliance. EU Annex 15 / USP <1079> ranges: 2-8°C for refrigerated, 15-25°C for controlled room temperature.
Important for hygroscopic products, biologics, and some oral solid dosage forms.
Less common; used where relative humidity isn't sufficient.
Air pressure for air-freight cold chain; cleanroom differential pressure.
Rare in pharma; more relevant for fragile medical-device transport.
Important for fragile pharma products and diagnostic equipment.
Inventory counts via sensor (rare at pack level; used in bulk inventory).
Pharmaceutical manufacturing process sensors (not typical for pack-level T&T).
entries — e.g., one device reports both temperature AND humidity in the same snapshot. Each report can carry a single value or aggregate statistics over an interval (
For pack-level cold-chain excursion detection, the platform evaluates each
against the bound product's temperature band (from the GTIN master data) at ingestion and propagates the excursion flag onto affected SGTINs.
Not every disposition is meaningful after every
. 'destroying' → 'active' makes no sense (destruction produces a destroyed object, not an active one). This section catalogues the canonical valid pairings for pharma supply chain events.

This is the single most useful reference for UI form validation, ingestion-pipeline business-rule validation, and state-machine design. The validation engine should use this matrix to produce helpful warnings when a combination looks unusual — not reject outright (CBV does not forbid unusual combinations), but flag for review.
New SGTIN commissioned at packaging line
Action = ADD. No prior events for this EPC. ILMD typically populated with batch + expiry.
Pack aggregated into case; case aggregated into pallet
Pallets staged at dock pending truck arrival
Primary shipping event; custody transfers to carrier
when the event's primary purpose is the reading.
Pallets received, custody transfers to receiver
if relying on upstream aggregation events (via
action = DELETE. SSCC's useful life ends here.
Pack held in warehouse / on shelf
Long-duration state. Often paired with periodic
Outcome disposition set based on inspection result.
Pulled samples go unavailable; rest of batch stays active.
OBSERVE. No status change unless discrepancy found.
Pack selected against an outbound order
Terminal for pharma. Action = DELETE. SGTIN's life ends.
OTC pack purchased at retail checkout
Pack returned from pharmacy to wholesaler
EU FMD allows re-dispense within 10 days of initial dispense; DSCSA saleable returns have specific verification.
Terminal. Action = DELETE. Often witnessed for controlled substances.
Datalogger glued to pallet; sensor mounted in cold room
UAE Licensed Agent repackages imported product
The following pairings are syntactically valid per CBV but semantically unusual. The platform should flag them (not reject) for ops review:
— destruction events should produce a destroyed disposition.
Any ADD action preceded by prior events on the same EPC
— ADD claims first life of an object.

This section documents the end-to-end state machine for a serialised pharma pack (SGTIN) through the CBV vocabularies, from commissioning at the packaging line to terminal state at dispensing, destruction, or recall.
The platform enforces these transitions at ingestion: an event whose (
, disposition) combination doesn't match a valid transition from the object's current state is flagged for review (QUARANTINED, not REJECTED).
Normal forward flow from manufacture to dispense:
When things don't go as planned — recalls, damages, quarantines:
may be re-dispensed if anti-tamper intact
Once a pack reaches a terminal state, no further business events are permitted (only
). Terminal states for a pharma SGTIN:
dispensed — the pack was handed to a
with later dispensed — split-dispense use case.
disposed — sent for disposal (waste, recycle) without formal destruction witness.
stolen — declared stolen; remains in this state until recovered and explicitly restored.
When the source of truth projects any of these dispositions onto the
transitions to DISPENSED / DESTROYED / STOLEN respectively and downstream ingestion blocks further state-changing events.
CBV is deliberately finite. Some concepts are not in CBV but arise in practice — industry-specific sensor types, region-specific
values, tenant-specific business transaction types. In those
a user vocabulary (a set of URIs outside the CBV namespace) is permitted.
The platform's policy for user-vocabulary values:
MUST be expressed as a URI with a non-CBV namespace (not https://ref.gs1.org/cbv/... and not
MUST be registered in the platform's per-tenant extension-vocabulary table for that tenant, along with a human-readable definition.
Unregistered user-vocabulary values ingested on an event → QUARANTINE + alert the ops team.

within an industry and GS1 eventually adopts it into CBV, the platform does a migration: the user-vocabulary URI is retired and replaced with the new CBV URI, with the old values remapped in the payload.

CBV is versioned. The latest ratified version is CBV 2.0 (June 2022), officially ISO/IEC 19988:2024. Between CBV versions, some values are added, deprecated, or clarified. Notable changes since CBV 1.0:
CBV 1.1 (2014): added class-level quantity in events; added
' for pharma; added disposition values 'dispensed' and '
'; added sensor measurement type vocabulary; added new disposition values (available,
, unavailable); introduced EPCIS Event Hash ID (
:///sha-256;...) as an event identifier; introduced GS1 Digital Link URI syntax alongside classic URN form.
' was DEPRECATED in CBV 2.0 (though still present in the JSON-LD context for backward compatibility). Do not use it in new events.
The ingestion pipeline runs the following CBV-related checks:
Value must be present in the registered CBV 2.0 vocabulary for that
be a registered user-vocabulary value for this tenant.
Action must be DELETE; disposition must be 'dispensed' or '
Action must be DELETE; disposition must be 'destroyed'.
Action must be ADD; disposition should be 'active'.
QUARANTINE if action mismatches; WARN if disposition differs
types must be from CBV vocabulary.
Must be a valid CBV ER-<name> URI. 'other' values permitted but flagged.
Must be a CBV sensor type. UoM must be a UN/CEFACT Rec 20 code.
WARN if type not in CBV list
Dispositions used persistently should typically be damaged / recalled / stolen / expired /
Must be registered in tenant extension vocabulary AND declared in the

User-vocabulary registration workflow: confirm whether tenants can self-register extension values through a portal, or whether every user-vocabulary addition requires platform-admin approval. Trade-off between tenant agility and audit traceability.

CBV-Compliant vs CBV-Compatible emission: confirm we DEFAULT to CBV-Compliant output (no custom URIs in our own-generated events). Some tenants may want CBV-Compatible output if their own internal systems require custom values.
' (deprecated in CBV 2.0) raise a deprecation warning at ingestion? Raise as WARN, ERROR, or silently accept? Recommended: WARN + log.
CBV version for output generation: confirm we emit CBV 2.0 URIs in our events. Some regulators and older trading partners still expect CBV 1.2 URNs — is there a per-partner output version?
Pairing-matrix enforcement: confirm the recommended default is WARN (not QUARANTINE) for unusual pairings. Too-strict enforcement may block legitimate edge cases; too-loose
Persistent-disposition usage: the platform distinguishes persistent-set, persistent-unset, and transient dispositions. Confirm the FE UX for displaying the combined picture ('this pack is transiently
AND persistently damaged') to an operator scanning its history.

Sensor type vocabulary: the list in Section 7 is the common subset. CBV 2.0 defines a broader set for food safety (microorganism concentrations, chemical residues) that pharma doesn't use. Confirm we carry only the pharma-relevant subset in our sensor-type validation list, rejecting unfamiliar ones as user vocabulary.
Industry-vertical extensions: GDST (seafood traceability) defines additional
, transshipment, landing). If the platform ever extends beyond pharma, these and their peers (tobacco TPD2, food FSMA 204) become relevant — scope for future CBV extension specs.

UI presentation of CBV vocabulary: confirm we render CBV values as the short name (e.g., 'shipping') in the UI and only show the full URI in developer/audit views. Short names are more readable; URIs preserve unambiguity.

Multi-language descriptions: CBV definitions are in English only (GS1 source). Confirm whether the platform translates CBV definitions for non-English operators (UAE = English + Arabic) — and if so, who owns the translation master. Recommended: maintain translations in the platform's master data, separate from the GS1 English source of truth.
